verificationNote: Requirements constructed from IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, and NACSA Act 854 obligations. Not endorsed by any standards body — verify against authoritative sources before use in formal assessments.
name: Identity and Access Management
description: Requirements for managing who can access OT systems, how they authenticate, and what they can do. OT IAM must balance security with operational accessibility — an operator who cannot access the HMI during an emergency poses a safety risk. Design for security under normal conditions and graceful degradation under emergency conditions.

requirements.name: Individual user accounts — elimination of shared accounts
requirements.description: Every person accessing OT systems must use an individual named account. Shared accounts ('operator', 'engineer', 'admin') must be eliminated from all OT systems within scope of the IEC 62443 assessment.

technical.summary: Individual accounts on HMI, SCADA, historian, engineering workstations. For PLCs and RTUs that cannot support individual accounts: individual accounts enforced at the jump server / PAM tool level — the gateway authenticates the individual even if the PLC only sees a shared service account.
actions: Inventory all shared accounts on OT systems
Create individual accounts for all OT users on HMI/SCADA/engineering tools
Implement PAM tool to control access to PLC programming interfaces (individual authentication at PAM, proxied to PLC shared account)
Disable or remove shared accounts after individual accounts operational
Document exceptions with compensating controls for systems that technically cannot support individual accounts

governance.summary: Account creation/deletion requires OT manager approval. Annual account access review.
actions: OT account management procedure
Annual access review — remove leavers and role-changers
Joiners/movers/leavers process includes OT system accounts

slMapping.sl2: Individual accounts on all OT workstations/servers; PAM for embedded devices
slMapping.sl3: No shared accounts anywhere; full individual accountability; PAM for all privileged access
slMapping.sl4: Hardware-bound identities for safety system access; biometric authentication for safety-critical functions

requirements.name: Role-based access control (RBAC) for OT
requirements.description: Define OT access roles aligned with operational functions and implement RBAC across all OT systems. Roles must reflect least-privilege — operators get process visibility and control within their area; engineers get programming access; administrators get full access only when needed.

technical.summary: Define OT roles: Operator (read + limited write for process area), Engineer (PLC programming + configuration), SCADA Admin (system configuration), Historian Admin (data management), Security Admin (security configuration only). Implement in SCADA/HMI and AD where integrated.
actions: Define OT role taxonomy (Operator, Engineer, SCADA Admin, Security Admin, Vendor)
Map each role to specific access rights per OT system
Implement RBAC in SCADA/DCS/HMI user management
Restrict engineering access to Engineering Workstation Zone — not from HMI workstations
Vendor accounts: most restricted role by default; elevated only for specific maintenance tasks

governance.summary: Role definitions reviewed annually. Role assignment requires OT manager approval. Privileged roles (Engineer, Admin) reviewed quarterly.
actions: Role definition document reviewed annually
Quarterly review of Engineer and Admin role assignments
Role assignment change records maintained

slMapping.sl2: Defined RBAC with least privilege; role review process
slMapping.sl3: Fine-grained RBAC; separation of duties for high-impact actions; PAM for privilege escalation
slMapping.sl4: JIT privilege elevation; dual-authorisation for safety-critical commands

requirements.name: Privileged access management for OT
requirements.description: Privileged accounts (engineering, admin, vendor) in OT must be managed through a Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution that provides credential vaulting, session recording, and just-in-time access provisioning.

technical.summary: PAM tool (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea) in IDMZ vaults credentials for OT privileged accounts. Engineers request session through PAM — never know the actual password. All sessions recorded.
actions: Deploy PAM tool for all OT privileged accounts
Vault all OT admin credentials (PLC programming accounts, SCADA admin accounts, historian admin, network device admin)
Configure session recording for all privileged OT sessions
Implement credential rotation after each privileged session (PLC service accounts)
Configure PAM to proxy connections to OT targets — privileged user never has direct network access to OT

governance.summary: PAM access requests require OT manager approval for sensitive operations. Emergency break-glass access documented and audited.
actions: PAM access request procedure defined
Break-glass emergency access procedure with mandatory post-use review
Monthly PAM usage audit report

evidenceItems: Vaulted credential register (account names — not passwords)

slMapping.sl2: Privileged accounts inventoried; password management policy
slMapping.sl3: PAM solution deployed with credential vaulting and session recording
slMapping.sl4: JIT credentials; break-glass with immediate alert; dual-approval for safety system access

requirements.name: Default credential elimination
requirements.description: All OT devices must have default vendor credentials changed before deployment or before network connectivity is established. This is the single highest-impact, lowest-cost security action in OT.

technical.summary: Change all default credentials at commissioning before network connection. Document changed credentials in PAM vault. Include in commissioning checklist.
actions: Create OT commissioning checklist including: change all default credentials, disable unused services, verify network configuration
Change default credentials on every OT device before connecting to OT network
Store new credentials only in PAM vault (never in spreadsheet, sticky notes, or email)
Verify default credentials are changed on all existing OT assets — active audit
For devices that cannot have credentials changed (some legacy PLCs): document, segment, and compensate

governance.summary: Default credential change is a mandatory commissioning step. Non-compliance requires OT manager waiver with compensating controls.
actions: Include default credential check in OT asset commissioning sign-off
Annual audit of OT assets for default credentials (using passive monitoring or config review)

evidenceItems: Commissioning checklist with default credential change sign-off

slMapping.sl1: Default credentials changed on accessible devices
slMapping.sl2: Default credentials changed on all devices before network connection; commissioning checklist enforced
slMapping.sl3: Annual verification audit; compensating controls for any exceptions
slMapping.sl4: Automated detection of default credentials via OT monitoring platform

requirements.name: Session management for OT
requirements.description: OT session management must balance security (automatic timeout of idle sessions) with operational safety (a timed-out session at a critical moment must not create a hazard). Timeouts must be tuned per application — an HMI operator screen timing out mid-process operation is an operational risk; an unattended engineering workstation with no timeout is a security risk.

legal.summary: IEC 62443 SR-2.5 requires session lock after a period of inactivity. SR-1.11 requires session termination for remote sessions. NACSA s18 requires measures appropriate to NCII sensitivity.

technical.summary: Session timeout policy applied per application type: Engineering workstations (15 min), SCADA admin consoles (30 min), HMI operator screens (configurable per operational procedure — may require operations engineering input). Remote sessions have shorter timeouts than local.
actions: Engineering workstations: 15-minute inactivity timeout with screen lock (not logout — preserves running tools but requires re-authentication)
SCADA administration consoles: 30-minute inactivity timeout
Remote access sessions (VPN/jump server): 60-minute maximum session duration; 30-minute idle timeout; automatic session termination with PAM session recording preserved
HMI operator screens: consult operations engineering — some operational contexts require persistent display; compensating control is physical access control to HMI room rather than screen timeout
Concurrent session limits: maximum 1 concurrent privileged session per account; alert on concurrent logins from different source IPs
Session termination: remote sessions terminated immediately on job completion via PAM (vendor should not have persistent connection)
Log all session events: login, logout, timeout, failed authentication, session termination

governance.summary: Session timeout policy reviewed annually. HMI timeout settings approved by operations engineering. Remote session completion confirmed via PAM session records.
actions: Annual session timeout policy review
HMI session configuration requires operations engineering sign-off
Monthly PAM session audit — verify sessions terminated after vendor work completed
Concurrent login alert review weekly

evidenceItems: OT session management policy by application type
PAM remote session timeout and termination configuration
Operations engineering sign-off on HMI session settings

slMapping.sl2: Session timeouts on engineering workstations and admin consoles; remote session limits
slMapping.sl3: Per-application timeout policy; concurrent session limits; automatic remote session termination; session audit
slMapping.sl4: Short-lived ephemeral sessions for privileged OT access; JIT session tokens that cannot persist beyond task scope

requirements.name: Emergency access — break-glass procedures
requirements.description: OT environments must have a tested emergency access procedure (break-glass) for situations where normal authentication fails: during a cyber incident that locks out legitimate users, hardware failure of the authentication system, or emergency requiring access by a person whose account is unavailable. Break-glass access must be tightly controlled, logged, and mandatory post-use reviewed.

legal.summary: IEC 62443 SR-1.10 requires that authenticator management includes procedures for authenticators that cannot be accessed. SR-2.1 requires least privilege while also ensuring that authorized users can access systems. NACSA s18 requires a balanced approach — emergency access that prevents safety incidents is part of appropriate cybersecurity measures.

technical.summary: Break-glass accounts: one per OT system (SCADA/DCS/historian/engineering tool), credentials in physical sealed envelope in locked cabinet, access to cabinet requires two-person approval, automatic alert on envelope open. All break-glass use is a mandatory security incident trigger.
actions: Define break-glass accounts for each OT system: local admin account on SCADA server, local admin on DCS engineering console, emergency recovery account on historian
Store break-glass credentials in physical sealed envelopes in locked cabinet (not in electronic PAM for emergency scenarios where PAM may be unavailable)
Cabinet access requires two-person approval: OT manager + security manager (or site manager for unmanned facilities)
Implement tamper-evident seals on envelopes — opening requires breaking the seal
Alert triggered when break-glass cabinet is opened (alarm, email, or physical notification to OT security team)
Break-glass use is always a mandatory security event: immediate notification to OT security manager; post-use review within 24 hours; credentials rotated after every use
Annual test of break-glass procedure: verify envelopes are present, seals intact, credentials work, post-use rotation procedure tested
Separate break-glass procedure for safety systems: SIS break-glass requires operations + safety manager approval and is documented in the SIS management of change procedure

governance.summary: Break-glass procedure approved by OT manager and security manager. Annual test. All break-glass use triggers mandatory security review. Post-use credential rotation enforced.
actions: Annual break-glass procedure test with documented results
Break-glass use incident log — every use documented with: who, why, when, what was done, credential rotation date
Credentials rotated after every use — rotation documented
Envelope seal inspection quarterly — confirm seals intact; reseal if compromised

evidenceItems: Break-glass credential storage cabinet configuration and access log

slMapping.sl2: Break-glass accounts defined; sealed envelopes in locked cabinet; post-use mandatory review
slMapping.sl3: Two-person approval; tamper-evident seals; automatic alert on access; credential rotation after every use; annual test
slMapping.sl4: Separate SIS break-glass requiring safety manager approval; dual-person SIS break-glass with physical sign-off; real-time alert to OT security team on access
